food_subgroup: Cooked pork dishes — fried or crispy pork

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Korean crispy fried pork slices; closest parent 'pork food product' (FOODON_03301116) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Pig meat products) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code for Korean-style crispy fried pork slices exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226934003 'Pork (substance)' is the closest available term; no specific concept for crispy fried pork slices exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Korean crispy pork is approximately 5 slices, weighing roughly 100 g, typically served as a side dish or appetiser in a Korean restaurant meal.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, fresh, belly (side), raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 10006); adapted for fried preparation using USDA frying retention factors
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Raw pork belly values from USDA SR Legacy (NDB 10006) were adapted to reflect deep-frying preparation. Fat content is increased to account for oil absorption during frying (approximately +5–8 g fat per 100 g). Sodium is elevated to reflect seasoning and marinade typical of Korean-style crispy pork. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. This is a medium-confidence estimate; no exact Korean crispy pork entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central branded foods.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021; analogous estimate based on fried pork / meat-based fried food category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean crispy pork slices. GI estimated at 40 based on the analogous category of lightly battered fried meat products, which are predominantly protein and fat with minimal carbohydrate. The starch coating contributes a small carbohydrate load. Glycemic load per 100 g serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g) / 100 = (40 × 4.5) / 100 = 1.8. This is a low GI food due to the dominant protein and fat matrix suppressing glycemic response. Basis: University of Sydney GI Database category guidance for meat-based fried foods and Atkinson et al. 2021 AJCN international GI tables.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g) from seasoning, marinade, and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 2–3 slices per serving, as the sodium from soy-based marinade and seasoning can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium targets of 1500–2000 mg; pairing with unseasoned vegetables can help offset sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High total fat and saturated fat content despite low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the glycemic index is low (~40) due to the dominant protein and fat matrix, the high total fat content (~34 g per 100 g) and saturated fat (~11.5 g) may contribute to insulin resistance over time; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion to 3–4 slices and balance with high-fibre vegetables to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~11.5 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (~78 mg per 100 g) from deep-fried pork belly
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high saturated fat and cholesterol content of deep-fried pork belly poses a meaningful cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia should restrict consumption to occasional small portions and prioritise leaner protein sources in their regular diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Korean crispy pork (5 slices) is a popular Korean restaurant dish consisting of thinly sliced pork — typically pork belly or pork shoulder — that has been marinated in a soy-garlic seasoning blend, coated in a light starch batter, and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The exterior is crunchy and well-seasoned, while the interior remains tender and juicy with a rich pork flavour. The dish is commonly served as a side dish (banchan) or appetiser in Korean cuisine, often accompanied by dipping sauces, pickled vegetables, or steamed rice. In Singapore, it appears on the menus of Korean casual dining restaurants such as Ajumma's. Nutritionally, crispy pork is energy-dense (~395 kcal per 100 g) and high in fat (~34 g), particularly saturated fat (~11.5 g), due to the pork belly cut and deep-frying method. Protein content is moderate (~17.5 g per 100 g). Carbohydrate content is low (~4.5 g), primarily from the starch coating, resulting in a low glycemic index (~40). Sodium is elevated (~480 mg per 100 g) from soy-based seasoning. Clinically, this dish is of moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium, moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to high fat content despite low GI, and high concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat and cholesterol. Portion control is recommended for individuals managing any of these conditions.

How much fat is in Korean fried pork?
Is Korean crispy pork high in sodium?
Korean crispy pork calories per slice

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Korean crispy pork slices was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values were adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw pork belly data (NDB 10006) with adjustments for deep-frying oil absorption and Korean-style seasoning. GI is an analogous estimate based on fried meat product category guidance from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Medium confidence reflects the adaptation and estimation steps required.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw pork belly (NDB 10006) with frying adjustments; no exact Korean crispy pork entry was available in approved primary sources. 2. Serving size of 100 g (5 slices) is an estimate based on typical restaurant portion weights for Korean crispy pork; actual weight may vary by restaurant. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork belly data and are not directly measured for this specific Korean preparation. 4. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record; all micronutrient fields have been populated with estimated values from the USDA SR Legacy source. 5. Allergen information (soy, gluten) is based on typical Korean crispy pork preparation; actual allergen content may vary by restaurant recipe. 6. Halal status is marked false as pork is not halal; kosher status is false as pork is not kosher. 7. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Korean crispy pork exists in the University of Sydney GI Database.

Populated aliases and aliases_by_language with Korean native script, romanized, and English variants; verified consistency between aliases_by_language and flat aliases list to satisfy Q8.

Ensured all per_100g values are numeric with no -1 sentinel values; documented estimation basis in notes to satisfy G3.
